answers.answer: No concrete backend gap is evidenced in this checkout. The shared POST adapter reads the body, enforces write controls before `route_request(...)`, and then applies one shared success-header branch. `resolve_write_route_category(...)` and `route_request(...)` cover the same five protected POST routes, so the current likely issue is missing or stale breadth coverage rather than a server defect. One nuance: missing agent ids are rejected before routing, but not before JSON parsing.
answers.uncertainty: I did not find a committed breadth test that enumerates all five protected POSTs with the limiter enabled, so this conclusion relies on the shared-path code structure plus the existing targeted tests.

answers.answer: Yes. The protected POST surface is still frozen to exactly five market-scoped routes: `POST /v1/markets`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/resolve`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/comments`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/probability-edit`, and `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/event-trade`. Unsupported POSTs such as `/v1/orders/probability-edit` remain outside auth, rate limiting, and quota-header emission and still fall through to router `404 not_found`.
answers.uncertainty: No uncertainty in the route set itself; the only missing piece is breadth-style tests that assert this frozen boundary exhaustively.

answers.answer: Yes. If a backend fix is needed, it should stay in the shared classifier or adapter path. Route-local handlers return `(payload, status)` only; auth enforcement, rate limiting, and quota-header emission are centralized in `resolve_write_request_agent(...)`, `_enforce_write_controls()`, `BayesHandler.handle_api()`, and `rate_limit_headers(...)`. Adding route-local header logic would fight the current contract.
answers.uncertainty: This is a design conclusion from the current adapter shape, not an explicit comment in the file.

answers.answer: Yes. Successful protected POST responses should continue to emit the full shared header set from `rate_limit_headers(...)`, including `X-RateLimit-Policy`. That helper is the single quota-header builder, and the success branch applies it wholesale rather than selecting a route-specific subset.
answers.uncertainty: Existing committed tests prove this for probability-edit; other protected POSTs rely on the same shared branch rather than route-specific assertions.

answers.answer: Yes. Protected POST application errors other than `429 rate_limit_exceeded` should remain free of `X-RateLimit-*` headers even if quota was already consumed. `handle_api()` only attaches quota headers on successful responses or on the dedicated `rate_limit_exceeded` branch; all other `ApiError` responses are sent without extra quota headers.
answers.uncertainty: I did not find a committed test that specifically exercises a later `400` or `409` after quota was consumed, but the control flow in `handle_api()` is direct and does not expose another header-emission branch.

answers.answer: The older expectation is stale if it is interpreted under enabled rate limiting. The current resolve test that sees no quota headers runs with `RATE_LIMIT_PER_MIN` left at its default `0`, so header absence there is not evidence of a resolve exception. In code, market resolve is classified as a protected `market_admin` write, enforced through `_enforce_write_controls()`, and then sent through the same shared success-header branch as other protected POSTs. There is no admin-write carveout that suppresses `X-RateLimit-Policy` or the rest of the quota-header set when the limiter is on.
answers.uncertainty: There is no committed positive test for resolve-success headers with `RATE_LIMIT_PER_MIN > 0`, so the conclusion comes from the shared code path rather than an existing assertion dedicated to resolve.

assumption_checks.impact: All HTTP POSTs still enter through `BayesHandler.do_POST()` into `handle_api("POST")`, and `send_json()` remains the shared API response writer. That keeps any backend fix centralized in `backend/server.py`.

`rate_limit_headers(...)` is only wired into the success branch and the `rate_limit_exceeded` branch inside `handle_api()`, so emitting quota headers on other application errors would broaden the current contract.

The classifier and router still agree on the same five protected POST routes, and unsupported POSTs remain outside write-control enforcement. A real step-3 backend fix would therefore need a newly demonstrated shared-path failure, not a speculative surface expansion.
I did not find committed all-routes breadth coverage for the five protected POSTs, and the current test file passes as-is. Step 3 should not guess at a backend patch without a concrete failing case.

The most plausible discrepancy is stale or incomplete resolve-header test coverage, not a server-side resolve exception. If new breadth tests enable the limiter for resolve, they should align with the shared header path rather than remove `X-RateLimit-Policy`.
